question.text: When an investor uses derivatives to protect an existing portfolio from adverse price movements, this is known as:

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Hedging is the use of derivatives to protect an open position in the underlying from the risks of future price movements. When the underlying portfolio has been built with a specific objective and unexpected price movements may place those objectives at risk, hedging helps manage this risk.
**The Trap:** Speculation is taking a derivative position without an underlying position, implementing a directional view at lower cost. Arbitrage is exploiting price differentials between two markets. Market making involves providing liquidity — none of these involve protecting an existing portfolio.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Derivatives basics overlap with Series VIII, XIII. The three uses of derivatives (hedging, speculation, arbitrage) are fundamental concepts tested across all derivatives exams.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Hedging is the most legitimate use of derivatives for investors. A portfolio manager who fears a market decline can hedge using index futures or put options, protecting the portfolio without having to sell the underlying stocks.
